title: The First Sunday after Christmas
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
December 26, 2021 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the First Sunday after Christmas Day, falling in the Christmas Season proper (which extends through January 5, 2022, ending with Epiphany). Under the Revised Common Lectionary (standard in most Episcopal parishes), the readings would likely be 1 Samuel 2:18-26 or Isaiah 61:10-62:3, Psalm 148, Colossians 3:12-17 or 1 John 1:1-7, and Luke 2:41-52 (the Holy Family in the Temple, also known as the Feast of the Holy Family in some traditions). The liturgical color is white, and the season emphasizes the incarnation and the mystery of God becoming human.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of its 80th General Convention held in July 2021 in Austin, Texas, with ongoing implementation of resolutions passed that summer, including significant actions on racial reconciliation and LGBTQ+ inclusion. The Diocese of Colorado had been operating under Bishop Kym Harris's leadership since her election in 2017, with no major episcopal transitions at this moment. No major denominational crises or controversies were dominating church news in this specific week, allowing parishes to focus on seasonal observance.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing post-holiday weather patterns typical for late December, with the city preparing for New Year's celebrations as the year 2021 drew to a close. The Colorado legislature was not in session, with the next regular session scheduled to begin in January 2022. Mayor Michael Hancock's administration was managing ongoing public health and homelessness concerns as the city emerged from the Delta variant surge of COVID-19, though cases were beginning to rise again due to the emerging Omicron variant.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant national story was the rapid spread of the Omicron COVID-19 variant, with case counts surging dramatically across the United States in late December 2021, leading to hospital strain and widespread testing shortages. President Biden faced criticism over his administration's pandemic response and the continued economic challenges including inflation, which had reached 6.8% year-over-year by December. The January 6 House Select Committee was actively investigating the Capitol riot with high-profile hearings scheduled for the coming weeks, and the Supreme Court had recently blocked Biden's vaccine mandate for large employers, creating ongoing political division.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia continued military buildup on the Ukrainian border with over 100,000 troops reported positioned for potential invasion, with Western nations warning of imminent conflict and diplomatic negotiations failing to produce breakthrough agreements. The Omicron variant was spreading globally with countries worldwide implementing new restrictions; the United Kingdom was experiencing record case numbers. North Korea had recently tested missiles, and tensions remained elevated on the Korean Peninsula as the year concluded.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in late December 2021 was marked by pandemic fatigue mixed with holiday traditions, though COVID uncertainty dampened the typical celebration mood compared to 2020; many people were navigating changed plans due to Omicron surges. Culturally, there was a sense of weariness and division after two years of pandemic disruption, with questions about whether normalcy would return in 2022. The recent release of "Spider-Man: No Way Home" dominated entertainment conversation, and the sports world was focused on college football bowl season and the NFL's final regular season games.
